title_en: Zakat, Fasting, and Hajj in Bidayat al-Mubtadi
title_id: Zakat, Puasa, dan Haji dalam Bidayat al-Mubtadi
content_id:
Pembahasan al-Marghinani tentang tiga pilar Islam yang tersisa dalam Bidayat al-Mubtadi mengikuti hukum-hukum mazhab Hanafi sebagaimana yang ditetapkan oleh Abu Hanifah, Abu Yusuf, dan Muhammad asy-Syaybani — tiga pilar tradisi ijtihad Hanafi.

Zakat dalam mazhab Hanafi berlaku untuk lima kategori harta: emas dan perak (atau mata uang), barang dagangan, ternak, hasil pertanian, dan harta terpendam atau kekayaan mineral (rikaz). Nisab emas adalah dua puluh dinar (sekitar 85 gram) dan perak dua ratus dirham (sekitar 595 gram). Sebuah posisi Hanafi yang patut dicatat adalah bahwa nisab untuk menentukan kewajiban zakat dapat dihitung menggunakan standar emas atau perak — dan mazhab Hanafi secara tradisional lebih menyukai standar perak karena menghasilkan ambang yang lebih rendah, dengan demikian membawa lebih banyak harta ke dalam kewajiban zakat dan memastikan distribusi yang lebih besar kepada kaum miskin.

Posisi mazhab Hanafi tentang zakat hasil pertanian lebih luas dari mazhab lain: zakat berlaku untuk semua tanaman dan produk pertanian, bukan hanya biji-bijian dan buah-buahan kering. Tanah yang diairi secara alami (oleh hujan atau sungai) dikenakan 10% dari hasilnya; tanah yang diairi oleh irigasi sebesar 5%. Secara unik di antara empat mazhab, posisi Hanafi tidak mensyaratkan produk pertanian untuk mencapai ambang nisab minimum — bahkan sejumlah kecil hasil pertanian terkena zakat. Bidayat al-Mubtadi menyatakan ini dengan jelas.

Untuk puasa, mazhab Hanafi berpendapat bahwa niat puasa Ramadan harus dibuat selambat-lambatnya pada tengah hari — sebuah posisi yang lebih longgar dari persyaratan mazhab Maliki untuk berniat malam sebelumnya, meskipun lebih ketat dari sekadar mensyaratkan niat pada satu titik sebelum membatalkan puasa. Dasar evidensial hukum Hanafi ini — bahwa tengah hari menandai 'awal' periode puasa dalam satu interpretasi — dibiarkan untuk Al-Hidayah yang menjelaskannya.

Pendekatan mazhab Hanafi terhadap hal-hal yang membatalkan puasa (muftirat) dirinci dalam Bidayat al-Mubtadi. Mazhab Hanafi membedakan antara tindakan-tindakan yang membatalkan puasa dan mensyaratkan baik mengqada' hari itu (qada') maupun kafarah — seperti makan, minum, atau senggama yang disengaja selama Ramadan — dan tindakan-tindakan yang membatalkan puasa dan hanya mensyaratkan mengqada' hari itu. Mazhab Hanafi juga berpendapat bahwa tindakan-tindakan tertentu membatalkan puasa yang mungkin tidak diakui oleh mazhab lain, termasuk: meneteskan cairan ke telinga atau hidung yang sampai ke tenggorokan, menggunakan supositoria, atau senggama tanpa ejakulasi.

Haji dalam Bidayat al-Mubtadi mengikuti analisis mazhab Hanafi tentang unsur-unsur wajib (fara'id), tindakan-tindakan yang diperlukan (wajibat), dan praktik-praktik yang dianjurkan (sunan) dari ibadah haji. Fara'id haji dalam mazhab Hanafi ada tiga: ihram, wukuf di Arafah, dan tawaf al-ifadah. Sa'i antara Safa dan Marwa diklasifikasikan sebagai wajib (perlu) daripada fard (wajib) dalam mazhab Hanafi — artinya peninggalannya yang disengaja adalah dosa dan memerlukan pengorbanan kompensasi (dam), tetapi hajinya sendiri tidak batal. Ini berbeda dari mazhab Maliki dan Syafi'i, yang mengklasifikasikan sa'i sebagai rukun (fard) haji.

Penyajian ringkas Bidayat al-Mubtadi tentang tiga pilar ini memberi pelajar peta esensial praktik Hanafi dalam ibadah, yang kemudian akan mereka pelajari jauh lebih mendalam melalui syarh Al-Hidayah. Pemadatan teks adalah fitur pedagogis, bukan keterbatasan — ia menyediakan kerangka yang diisi syarh dengan penalaran, bukti, dan perbandingan antar-mazhab.
content_en:
Al-Marghinani's treatment of the remaining three pillars of Islam in Bidayat al-Mubtadi follows the Hanafi school's rulings as established by Abu Hanifah, Abu Yusuf, and Muhammad ash-Shaybani — the three pillars of the Hanafi juristic tradition.

zakah in the Hanafi school applies to five categories of wealth: gold and silver (or currency), trade goods, livestock, agricultural produce, and buried treasure or mineral wealth (rikaz). The nisab for gold is twenty dinars (approximately 85 grams) and for silver two hundred dirhams (approximately 595 grams). A notable Hanafi position is that the nisab for determining the zakah obligation may be calculated using either gold or silver — and the Hanafi school traditionally favored the silver standard because it results in a lower threshold, thereby bringing more wealth into the zakah obligation and ensuring greater distribution to the poor.

The Hanafi school's position on zakah for agricultural produce is broader than the other schools: zakah applies to all crops and agricultural products, not only grains and dried fruits. Any land that is watered naturally (by rain or rivers) is taxed at 10% of its produce; land watered by irrigation at 5%. Uniquely among the four schools, the Hanafi position does not require the produce to reach a minimum nisab threshold — even a small amount of agricultural produce is subject to zakah. Bidayat al-Mubtadi states this clearly.

For fasting, the Hanafi school holds that the intention for Ramadan fasting must be made by midday at the latest — a position more lenient than the Maliki school's requirement of intending the night before, though stricter than simply requiring intention at some point before breaking the fast. The evidential basis for this Hanafi ruling — that the middle of the day marks the 'beginning' of the fasting period in one interpretation — is left for Al-Hidayah to explain.

The Hanafi school's approach to things that break the fast (muftirat) is detailed in Bidayat al-Mubtadi. The Hanafi school distinguishes between acts that break the fast and require both making up the day (qada') and expiation (kaffara) — such as intentional eating, drinking, or sexual intercourse during Ramadan — and acts that break the fast and require only making up the day. The Hanafi school also holds that certain acts break the fast that other schools may not recognize, including: applying drops to the ear or nose that reach the throat, using a suppository, or sexual intercourse without ejaculation.

Hajj in Bidayat al-Mubtadi follows the Hanafi school's analysis of the obligatory elements (fara'id), necessary acts (wajibat), and recommended practices (sunan) of pilgrimage. The Hanafi school's fara'id of hajj are three: ihram, standing at Arafah, and tawaf al-ifadah. The sa'y between Safa and Marwa is classified as wajib (necessary) rather than fard (obligatory) in the Hanafi school — meaning its deliberate omission is sinful and requires a compensatory sacrifice (dam), but the hajj itself is not invalid. This differs from the Maliki and Shafi'i schools, which classify sa'y as a pillar (rukn) of hajj.

Bidayat al-Mubtadi's concise presentation of these three pillars gives students the essential map of Hanafi practice in worship, which they will then study in far greater depth through Al-Hidayah's commentary. The text's compression is a pedagogical feature, not a limitation — it provides the framework that the commentary fills with reasoning, evidence, and inter-school comparison.
content_ar: Al-Marghinani's treatment of the remaining three pillars of الإسلام in Bidayat al-Mubtadi follows the الحنفي school's rulings as established by Abu Hanifah, Abu يوسف ﷺ, and محمد ﷺ ash-Shaybani — the three pillars of the الحنفي juristic التراث. الزكاة in the الحنفي school applies to five categories of الثروة: gold and silver (or currency), trade goods, livestock, agricultural produce, and buried treasure or mineral الثروة (rikaz). The nisab for gold is twenty dinars (approximately 85 grams) and for silver two hundred dirhams (approximately 595 grams). A notable الحنفي position is that the nisab for determining the الزكاة الالتزام may be calculated using either gold or silver — and the الحنفي school traditionally favored the silver standard لأن it results in a lower threshold, thereby bringing more الثروة into the الزكاة الالتزام and ensuring greater distribution to the poor. The الحنفي school's position on الزكاة for agricultural produce is broader than the other schools: الزكاة applies to all crops and agricultural products, not only grains and dried fruits. Any land that is watered naturally (by rain or rivers) is taxed at 10% of its produce; land watered by irrigation at 5%. Uniquely among the four schools, the الحنفي position does not require the produce to reach a minimum nisab threshold — even a small amount of agricultural produce is subject to الزكاة. Bidayat al-Mubtadi states this clearly. For الصيام, the الحنفي school holds that the intention for Ramadan الصيام must be made by midday at the latest — a position more lenient than the المالكي school's requirement of intending the night قبل, though stricter than simply requiring intention at some point قبل breaking the fast. The evidential basis for this الحنفي ruling — that the middle of the day marks the 'beginning' of the الصيام period in one interpretation — is left for Al-Hidayah to explain. The الحنفي school's approach to things that break the fast (muftirat) is detailed in Bidayat al-Mubtadi. The الحنفي school distinguishes between acts that break the fast and require both making up the day (القضاء') and expiation (kaffara) — مثل intentional eating, drinking, or sexual intercourse خلال Ramadan — and acts that break the fast and require only making up the day. The الحنفي school أيضاً holds that certain acts break the fast that other schools may not recognize, بما في ذلك: applying drops to the ear or nose that reach the throat, using a suppository, or sexual intercourse without ejaculation. الحج in Bidayat al-Mubtadi follows the الحنفي school's analysis of the obligatory elements (fara'id), necessary acts (wajibat), and recommended practices (sunan) of pilgrimage. The الحنفي school's fara'id of الحج are three: ihram, standing at Arafah, and tawaf al-ifadah.
